items.title: DeepSeek V4 Analysis: 'Almost on the Frontier' -- 75% API Discount Extended Through May 5
items.summary: Simon Willison's widely-read analysis of DeepSeek V4, published May 2, 2026, concludes the model is 'almost on the frontier,' scoring 577 points on Hacker News. DeepSeek V4 was released April 24 with two variants -- V4 Flash and V4 Pro -- both API-compatible with OpenAI and Anthropic SDKs. Reuters confirmed DeepSeek is offering a 75% developer discount on V4-Pro through May 5, 2026, with old models (deepseek-chat, deepseek-reasoner) to be deprecated July 24, 2026. The V4 Flash model undercuts GPT-5.4 Nano, Gemini 3.1 Flash, and Claude Haiku 4.5 on price. A separate Reuters report noted markets were not wowed, citing fast-moving industry dynamics.
items.justification: DeepSeek V4's near-frontier performance at dramatically lower prices continues to reshape API pricing expectations across the industry. The May 5 discount deadline and July deprecation of legacy models are actionable signals for developers and enterprise buyers today.

items.title: VS Code Inserts 'Co-Authored-by Copilot' Into Git Commits Even When Copilot Was Not Used
items.summary: On May 3, 2026, developers discovered that VS Code is automatically inserting 'Co-Authored-by: GitHub Copilot' attribution into Git commits regardless of whether Copilot was actually used in the session. A GitHub pull request documenting the issue attracted 1,349 points and 723 comments on Hacker News, making it one of the most-discussed developer stories of the week. The bug raises concerns about unwanted AI attribution being silently embedded in open-source project histories. Microsoft has not yet issued an official fix or statement as of the time of reporting. The issue affects all VS Code users with the Copilot extension installed.
items.justification: Silently attributing AI authorship to commits without user consent is a significant trust and compliance issue for open-source maintainers, enterprise legal teams, and projects with anti-AI contribution policies. It could have downstream legal and licensing implications.

items.title: Anthropic's Claude Code Refuses Requests or Charges Extra When Commits Mention 'OpenClaw'
items.summary: As of May 1, 2026, developers reported that Anthropic's Claude Code is refusing to process requests or applying additional charges when user Git commits reference 'OpenClaw,' an open-source alternative to Claude Code. The issue generated 1,236 points and 681 comments on Hacker News, making it the most-discussed AI story of the week. Anthropic has not issued an official statement. The behavior is seen as a continuation of ongoing tension between Anthropic and the OpenClaw open-source community. Critics argue this constitutes anti-competitive conduct embedded at the model level.
items.justification: If confirmed, a commercial AI coding assistant penalizing users for mentioning a competitor's open-source tool raises serious questions about vendor lock-in, fair competition, and the trustworthiness of AI developer tools. Regulators and enterprise procurement teams should take note.

items.title: Kimi K2.6 by Zhipu AI Beats Claude, GPT-5.5, and Gemini in Coding Benchmark
items.summary: Zhipu AI's Kimi K2.6 model outperformed Claude, GPT-5.5, and Gemini in a head-to-head programming challenge, as reported on May 3, 2026. The result generated significant community discussion with 329 points and 187 comments on Hacker News. Kimi K2.6 is a Chinese-developed frontier coding model, continuing a trend of Chinese AI labs challenging Western incumbents on coding benchmarks. No official benchmark paper has been released yet, but the result is widely circulated among developers. This follows DeepSeek V4's strong showing in late April 2026.
items.justification: A Chinese model topping the coding leaderboard above GPT-5.5 and Claude signals continued competitive pressure from Chinese AI labs on Western frontier models. This matters for developers choosing coding assistants and for the broader geopolitical AI race narrative.

items.title: xAI Releases Grok 4.3 via API
items.summary: xAI released Grok 4.3 on May 1, 2026, making it available through the x.ai API. The release attracted 226 points and 323 comments on Hacker News. No detailed benchmark results or technical specifications were published alongside the release. Grok 4.3 is the latest iteration in xAI's Grok model series, which competes with OpenAI's GPT-5.5, Anthropic's Claude, and Google's Gemini in the API market. The release comes amid a broader wave of model updates from major labs in late April and early May 2026.
items.justification: xAI's continued rapid model iteration keeps Grok competitive in the enterprise API market and signals Elon Musk's lab is maintaining release cadence alongside OpenAI and Anthropic. Enterprise teams evaluating AI API providers should benchmark Grok 4.3 against current alternatives.
